skill.name: biopython
skill.description: Use Biopython reference guidance for sequence analysis, NCBI database access, BLAST workflows, structure parsing, alignments, and phylogenetics in Python.
skill.summary: Biopython guidance for molecular biology analysis in Python.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Manual review found documentation for legitimate Biopython workflows, not hidden executable skill behavior. Static critical heuristics are unsupported because the flagged patterns are markdown examples, placeholder API key text, NCBI service calls, local BLAST or DSSP wrappers, and ordinary file I/O. Publish with a warning that generated workflows may contact external biological databases, write local files, or invoke installed scientific command-line tools when the user runs them.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Network Access to Scientific Databases
medium_findings.description: The documentation teaches NCBI BLAST, Entrez fetch, and PDB download workflows. These are legitimate Biopython use cases, but running generated code can send biological sequences or identifiers to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly document network-backed Biopython calls. The risk is contextual because no hidden network call runs during skill loading, but user-run examples can transmit data.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Local File and Executable Workflows
medium_findings.description: The references include examples that write result files and invoke installed BLAST or DSSP tooling. These operations are expected for bioinformatics, but generated code should avoid untrusted paths and require user approval before running local binaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly write files and call Biopython command-line wrappers. They are not malicious by themselves, but they carry normal filesystem and external tool execution risk when copied into a workflow.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many Ruby or shell backtick findings, but reviewed locations are markdown code fences and command examples, not executable skill code. No install hook, script file, or automatic command execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples inside markdown fences. This strongly supports a false positive for automatic Ruby or shell execution.

low_findings.title: Placeholder API Key Is Not a Committed Secret
low_findings.description: The API key references use placeholder text for NCBI rate limits. This is not credential theft or a leaked secret, but users should be guided to store real keys outside shared source files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The value is a generic placeholder and appears in setup documentation. There is no evidence that a real key is bundled or sent to an unauthorized endpoint.

low_findings.title: Static Critical Combination Heuristic Is Unsupported
low_findings.description: The scanner combined documentation examples for network access, filesystem operations, placeholder credentials, and local scientific tools into a critical heuristic. Manual review found no prompt injection, no obfuscation, no exfiltration endpoint, and no hidden runtime behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple risk categories are present, but only as transparent documentation for the advertised Biopython domain. The absence of executable package code lowers confidence in any malicious interpretation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Calls in Generated Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use NCBI web services and PDB downloads. Treat sequence data and identifiers as potentially sensitive before running those workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and domain-appropriate. The main concern is user data exposure to third-party scientific services.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Binary Invocation Through Biopython Wrappers
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples run local BLAST and DSSP wrappers. Generated code should validate inputs and paths before invoking installed scientific binaries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command wrapper calls are documented and expected, with fixed example arguments. Risk depends on how future generated code fills paths and parameters.

content.value_statement: Bioinformatics work often requires accurate Python patterns for sequences, files, databases, BLAST, structures, and trees. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused Biopython guidance for reproducible molecular biology workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides sequence creation, translation, reverse complement, and SeqRecord manipulation with Biopython.
Shows how to read, write, and convert FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, PDB, mmCIF, and alignment files.
Explains NCBI Entrez searches, record fetching, PubMed queries, and rate-limit setup.
Documents online and local BLAST workflows, result parsing, filtering, and batch searches.
Covers protein structure parsing, DSSP usage, residue analysis, and phylogenetic tree construction.
Provides reference patterns for motifs, population genetics, and advanced sequence utilities.
limitations: It provides reference guidance and examples; it does not bundle Biopython or scientific databases.
Network examples require user credentials, NCBI compliance, and internet access when run.
Local BLAST and DSSP examples require separate command-line tools installed by the user.
Scientific results still need domain review, input validation, and reproducibility checks.

use_cases.title: Convert and Summarize Sequence Files
use_cases.description: Create Python scripts that parse FASTA, GenBank, or FASTQ files, summarize records, and convert formats for downstream tools.

use_cases.title: Query NCBI and Parse Records
use_cases.description: Build Entrez workflows that search PubMed or GenBank, fetch records, and extract publication or sequence metadata.

use_cases.title: Compare Sequences and Build Trees
use_cases.description: Generate alignment, BLAST, and phylogenetic analysis workflows with clear parameters and reusable output handling.

prompt_templates.title: Parse a FASTA File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython skill to help me read a FASTA file, count records, calculate sequence lengths, and summarize GC content.

prompt_templates.title: Convert GenBank to FASTA
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython skill to create a safe workflow that converts GenBank records to FASTA and preserves useful record identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch NCBI Records
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython skill to search NCBI Entrez for a gene, fetch matching nucleotide records, and document rate-limit settings.

prompt_templates.title: Build a BLAST Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Biopython skill to design a BLAST workflow that runs a query, parses XML results, filters hits, and fetches top sequences.

output_examples.input: A user asks for help summarizing a FASTA file with hundreds of sequences.
output_examples.output: The assistant produces a concise workflow that reads records as an iterator, reports counts and lengths, calculates GC content, and notes memory considerations.

output_examples.input: A user wants PubMed records for a disease-related search term.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains Entrez email setup, search parameters, summary retrieval, rate limits, and how to save reproducible query metadata.

output_examples.input: A user needs to compare a sequence against local BLAST databases.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines prerequisites, input validation, result parsing, filtering by E-value, and safe handling of output files.

best_practices: Confirm file formats, sequence alphabets, and record counts before running large analyses.
Use Entrez email and API keys responsibly, and avoid hardcoding real secrets in shared files.
Cache downloaded records and document parameters such as databases, thresholds, and genetic codes.
anti_patterns: Do not submit sensitive or unpublished sequence data to external services without approval.
Do not run generated local BLAST or DSSP commands with untrusted paths or unchecked input.
Do not assume biological conclusions are valid without checking data quality and method limits.

faq.question: Does this skill install Biopython?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must install Biopython and required scientific tools separately.

faq.question: Can it access NCBI databases?
faq.answer: It can guide code that uses Bio.Entrez or NCBI BLAST. Running that code may contact external NCBI services.

faq.question: Does it include real API keys?
faq.answer: No. The documentation uses placeholders and recommends NCBI API keys only for higher rate limits.

faq.question: Can it parse protein structures?
faq.answer: Yes. The references cover PDB and mmCIF parsing, residue inspection, DSSP workflows, and structure quality checks.

faq.question: Can it run local BLAST?
faq.answer: It can guide Biopython wrapper code for local BLAST. Users need BLAST+ tools and local databases installed.

faq.question: Is this suitable for clinical decisions?
faq.answer: No. It supports research workflows only. Clinical or diagnostic use requires validated pipelines and expert review.
